title: Capstone Workshop: Evaluate, Disposition, and Document
narrationScript: This capstone is one job, start to finish. A nozzle attachment weld on a pressure vessel built to ASME Section Eight, Division One. The method comes from Section Five, Article Six. The acceptance criteria come from Mandatory Appendix Eight. Your written procedure ties them together. Step zero comes before any interpretation: prove the test was valid. Right procedure revision, qualified materials with batch numbers logged, parameters inside their windows, and at least one hundred foot candles of white light, measured, never assumed. Then interpret every indication: false, nonrelevant, or relevant. A smear that wipes away and stays away is false. A line tracing the pad faying edge is geometry, nonrelevant. Whatever remains is measured at the specified development time and classified by the three to one rule. A toe indication a quarter inch long by a sixteenth wide is linear, and the pattern permits none. The weld is rejected, the defect routes through the quality system, and the repaired area reprocesses from step zero. Last comes the record: complete, traceable, corrected without erasure, and able to stand alone.

sections.heading: The Capstone Brief: One Weld, Start to Finish
sections.body:
This workshop is the whole Level II job compressed into one part. On the bench: a carbon-steel **nozzle attachment weld** — nozzle N-2, set through the shell of a new pressure vessel and reinforced with a pad — ready for its required penetrant examination.

The package that arrives with it is the usual stack, and every document in it has a defined job:

- The **purchase order** invokes the code of construction — **ASME BPVC Section VIII, Division 1** — which requires PT of this finished weld and supplies the acceptance standard: **Mandatory Appendix 8**.
- Section VIII sends the *how* to **ASME Section V, Article 6**, the method document.
- The shop's **written procedure PT-204 Rev C** — Level III approved — translates both into bench instructions: Type II visible penetrant, Method C solvent-removable, form e nonaqueous developer.

Two deliverables leave this bench, and the capstone grades you on both: a **disposition** you can defend line by line against the flowed-down criteria, and a **record** complete enough to stand alone in an audit two years from now. Work the cycle in order — verify, interpret, measure and classify, evaluate, disposition, document — and neither deliverable is ever in doubt.

sections.caption: The capstone cycle: three document chains feed six steps, and every rejected-then-repaired area re-enters at Step 0 for full reprocessing to the same criteria.

sections.heading: Step Zero: Prove the Test Was Valid
sections.body:
Acceptance criteria mean nothing applied to a bad test, so evaluation starts before you look at a single indication. Run the validity audit:

- **The document.** The bench copy of PT-204 is **Rev C** — the revision on file — and the acceptance standard it cites matches the contract.
- **The materials.** Penetrant, solvent remover, and developer are the products the procedure names, in date, with **batch numbers logged** on the report. (On a nickel-alloy, austenitic stainless, or titanium job you would also pull the certifications limiting sulfur and halogens; this shell is carbon steel.)
- **The process.** Part surface temperature sat inside the standard **40–125 °F** window. Penetrant dwell met the procedure's 10-minute minimum and the surface was never allowed to dry. Removal was a dry-cloth wipe followed by a **solvent-dampened** cloth — flushing the surface with solvent is prohibited, because it strips penetrant straight out of flaws. Developer went on as a thin, even film and dried to a uniform white.
- **The light.** Visible PT requires at least **100 fc (about 1,000 lx)** of white light at the examination surface. You measured 112 fc with a calibrated meter and recorded the reading, the meter's serial number, and its calibration due date.
- **The clock.** On this code pattern, final interpretation falls inside the reading window — **10 to 60 minutes** after the developer film dries.

Any miss on this list has one remedy — clean and reprocess — and the miss itself gets documented. Only a valid test earns the right to be interpreted.

sections.heading: Interpret First: False, Nonrelevant, Relevant
sections.body:
Five indications are showing. Interpretation sorts them **before** any measuring, and on a solvent-removable job your confirming tool is the **wipe-and-redevelop check**: remove the indication with a solvent-dampened swab, redevelop, and see whether it returns.

- **A** — a soft smear on the shell plate, well away from the weld. It wipes off and does not return: **false**, a process artifact — dye transferred from a glove or rag, not a discontinuity. Re-examine the area; there is nothing to evaluate.
- **B** — an unbroken line that traces the unwelded faying edge between the reinforcing pad and the shell, exactly. Real bleed-out, but its cause is **part configuration**: the crevice at a faying surface holds penetrant by design. **Nonrelevant** — noted on the report with its cause, not evaluated against flaw limits.
- **C** — a sharp line at the weld toe near the 3 o'clock position. It returns after wipe and redevelopment, crisp and growing: **relevant**. It gets measured.
- **D** — a single round spot on the weld cap: **relevant**, measured next.
- **E** — a pinpoint near the top of the weld, about 1/32 in. across. Real, but under the Appendix 8 pattern an indication with a major dimension of **1/16 in. (1.5 mm) or less is not relevant** — it never reaches evaluation.

When interpretation is genuinely in doubt, the answer is re-examination or full reprocessing of the area — never a shrug in either direction. And the wipe check is a *diagnostic*, not an eraser: an indication that keeps returning is real, no matter how many times a cloth can make it vanish for a minute.

sections.caption: The developed surface of nozzle weld N-2 at reading time. Work each indication — A through E — through interpretation, classification, and evaluation before touching the disposition block.

sections.heading: Measure, Classify, Evaluate — Then Disposition
sections.body:
Relevant indications are measured **as they appear at the specified development time**, in the units of the governing document — and you measure the **indication, not the flaw**. Appendix 8 is explicit that the size of the indication is the basis for acceptance evaluation; bleed-out wider than the crack feeding it counts against the part, never for it.

- **C** — 1/4 in. long by 1/16 in. wide. Length over width is 4, past the 3:1 boundary, so C is a **relevant linear indication**. The Appendix 8 pattern permits none. **Reject.**
- **D** — 1/8 in. across, length no more than three times width: **relevant rounded**. The rounded limit is 3/16 in. (5 mm); 1/8 in. is under it. **Accept and record.** One more check before moving on: D stands alone — no four or more rounded indications in a line spaced 1/16 in. or less edge to edge, which would reject on the alignment rule.
- **E** — major dimension 1/16 in. or less: not relevant, recorded only as the procedure requires.

The weld's disposition is the worst of its indications: **rejected**, because C exceeds the criteria and is therefore a **defect** — the word means rejectable by definition, so the report uses it for C and for nothing else. The weldment is status-marked, segregated, and routed through the quality system as a nonconformance. After weld repair, the area returns to **Step 0**: completely reprocessed — clean, penetrant, removal, developer — and evaluated to the same criteria. A fresh coat of developer over the old process is not a re-examination.

sections.title: Write the disposition the way an auditor will read it
sections.body: The results block cites **document, edition, and requirement**: "Rejected — relevant linear indication at the weld toe, 3 o'clock: ASME Section VIII, Div. 1, Mandatory Appendix 8 (contract edition) — no relevant linear indications permitted." "Per code" decides nothing and defends nothing. And two stop conditions end processing instead of producing a disposition: **no acceptance criteria in the package** — stop and obtain written direction from the cognizant engineering organization; and **conflicting documents** — the contract chain governs, and the conflict is resolved in writing before parts move.

sections.heading: The Record: If It Is Not Written Down, It Did Not Happen
sections.body:
The disposition is half the deliverable; the **record** is the other half. On code work, Section V, Article 6 requires the examination to be documented, and the employer's quality system defines the form. Whatever the form, a complete PT record carries:

- **Identification** — report number and date, part number and serial, material, and the manufacturing stage at which the examination ran.
- **Governing documents** — procedure and revision (PT-204 Rev C), method standard, acceptance standard with edition.
- **Materials** — manufacturer, designation, and **batch numbers** for penetrant, remover, and developer.
- **Process as run** — the measured values, not the planned ones: surface temperature, dwell, removal technique, developer form and development time, and the **light reading with the meter's serial number and calibration due date**.
- **Results** — the indication map and one log line per indication: location, size, interpretation, classification, disposition — plus the overall disposition with its criteria citation.
- **People** — examiner's name, certification (NDT Level II, PT), signature, and date, with review per the quality system.

Then the rules that keep it evidence: entries are made at the time of the work, in permanent form; corrections are a **single line through the error, the correct entry, initials, and the date** — never erasure, never white-out, never an obscured original. The finished test is simple and brutal: a qualified stranger, holding only this record, reaches the same disposition you did. That is what *stand-alone* means, and it is the standard this capstone grades.

sections.caption: Anatomy of the finished report: every block answers an auditor's question before it is asked — traceability, measured values, one row per indication, a cited disposition, and a clean signature trail.

interactive.title: Reject the Right Indication
interactive.intro: The developed weld map from this lesson — five indications, A through E, with the observations in the side cards. Exactly one of them rejects this weld under the ASME Section VIII, Division 1, Appendix 8 pattern. Click it.

regions.label: Indication A — smear on the shell plate
regions.feedback: A wipes off with a solvent-dampened cloth and never returns — a false indication left by handling or residue. It is not caused by a discontinuity, so it cannot reject anything. Re-examine the area and move on.

regions.label: Indication B — line at the pad faying edge
regions.feedback: B traces the unwelded faying edge between pad and shell exactly — real bleed-out, but explained by part configuration. Nonrelevant: note it and its cause on the report; it is not evaluated against the flaw limits.

regions.label: Indication C — sharp line at the weld toe
regions.feedback: Correct. At 1/4 in. by 1/16 in. the length-to-width ratio is 4:1 — past the 3:1 boundary — so C is a relevant linear indication, and the Appendix 8 pattern permits none. The weld is rejected; C is a defect and routes through the quality system.

regions.label: Indication D — round spot on the weld cap
regions.feedback: D is relevant — but at 1/8 in., with length no more than three times width, it classifies as rounded, and rounded indications reject only above 3/16 in. D is recorded and accepted.

regions.label: Indication E — pinpoint near the top of the weld
regions.feedback: E's major dimension is 1/16 in. or less, so under the Appendix 8 pattern it never reaches evaluation — not relevant by size. Record it only if the procedure requires.

checkQuestions.question: You arrive at the bench and the developed weld is already showing indications. Per the capstone cycle, what is your first action before interpreting anything?
options: Begin measuring the largest indication while the bleed-out is fresh
Wipe every indication and redevelop to see which ones return
Confirm the test itself was valid — procedure revision at the bench, in-date materials with batches logged, parameters inside their windows, and the white-light reading on record
Photograph the indications so the reading window cannot expire
checkQuestions.explanation: Acceptance criteria mean nothing applied to a bad test. Step 0 verifies the governing revision, the qualified materials, the process parameters, and the measured 100 fc minimum before any indication is interpreted — and a failed check means reprocess, not rationalize.

checkQuestions.question: Indication B is an unbroken line of bleed-out that exactly traces the unwelded faying edge between the reinforcing pad and the shell. How do you interpret it?
options: Nonrelevant — real bleed-out caused by part configuration, noted on the report with its cause
False — a linear indication that long can only be lint or residue
Relevant linear — it exceeds the 3:1 ratio, so the weld is rejected
A defect — every unbroken line is a crack until proven otherwise
checkQuestions.explanation: A nonrelevant indication is genuine bleed-out explained by the part's geometry — here, the crevice at a faying surface that holds penetrant by design. It is interpreted, explained, and noted, but it is not evaluated against the flaw acceptance limits.

checkQuestions.question: Indication C at the weld toe measures 1/4 in. long by 1/16 in. wide at the specified development time and returns after a solvent wipe and redevelopment. Under the Appendix 8 pattern, what is the call?
options: Accept — it is shorter than the 3/16 in. limit
Reject — 4:1 length-to-width makes it a relevant linear indication, and the pattern permits none
Not relevant — indications at a weld toe are caused by geometry
Wipe and redevelop until it stops returning, then accept
checkQuestions.explanation: 1/4 in. divided by 1/16 in. is 4 — past the 3:1 boundary — so C is linear, and the Appendix 8 pattern permits no relevant linear indications. The 3/16 in. limit applies only to rounded indications, and wiping a returning indication into silence is falsification, not inspection.

checkQuestions.question: The welder argues that the actual crack beneath indication C is probably shorter than the bleed-out, so the weld should pass. Why does that argument fail?
options: Because bleed-out is always exactly twice the flaw size, so the measurement is simply divided by two
It does not fail — the inspector should measure the flaw itself with a magnifier
Because penetrant indications can only be confirmed by radiography
Because Appendix 8 evaluates the size of the indication itself — if the indication exceeds the criteria, the part is rejectable
checkQuestions.explanation: The indication, measured as it appears at the specified development time, is the basis for acceptance evaluation. Bleed-out is normally wider than the discontinuity feeding it, and the criteria are written around that fact — there is no credit for the flaw underneath being smaller.

checkQuestions.question: While writing the report you enter the wrong light-meter reading. What does a compliant correction look like?
options: A single line through the error, the correct value beside it, your initials, and the date
White-out over the error so the finished record stays clean
Rewrite the report from scratch and shred the original
Erase the entry — inspection records are kept in pencil for exactly this reason
checkQuestions.explanation: Inspection records are permanent, contemporaneous evidence. A correction must leave the original entry legible — single line, correct entry, initials, date. Erasures, white-out, and destroyed originals break the audit trail and turn a record into a liability.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.6 and §4.1–4.3 (evaluation of indications; procedures, documentation, and standards — applied practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 6, liquid penetrant examination (written procedure, processing limits, and examination documentation)
ASME BPVC Section VIII, Division 1, Mandatory Appendix 8 (acceptance standards for PT indications)
ASTM E1417/E1417M, Standard Practice for Liquid Penetrant Testing (process requirements and acceptance flow-down context)
